evidences.title: SPCE Stock Whipsaws As Virgin Galactic Reshapes Balance Sheet
evidences.content: ## Why Traders Are Locked In On SPCE Volatility SPCE has turned into a volatility magnet. The daily chart shows a rocket ride from $2.47 on 2026/05/20 to a high close of $7.52 on 2026/06/01, followed by sharp pullbacks and another spike to $5.73 on 2026/06/11. That is the kind of wild range that short-term traders hunt, but it also punishes anyone who chases late. Intraday action tells the same story. On the most recent premarket tape, SPCE traded as high as a little above $6 before fading toward the low $5s. The five‑minute candles show repeated pops into the $6.15–$6.20 area, then steady selling, a classic pattern of momentum players taking profits while slower money buys the top. [...] Add in the $40.21M mixed securities shelf and ongoing at-the-market offerings, and traders see a steady supply of shares hanging over every rally. Combine that with Wallstreetbets buzz and you get exactly what we’ve seen: huge squeezes up, brutal reversals down, and price action that often ignores fundamentals in the short term. ## Conclusion For active traders, SPCE sits at the crossroads of dream and dilution. Virgin Galactic has laid out a tight 2026 roadmap: Q3 flight tests, Q4 commercial s...

evidences.content: until cash flow turns positive. So, everything has to go right. If everything goes right, this could be a multibagger stock. It could go to 10, 20 dollars. No question. But the bare case, the value investors view. The bare case is just looking at the facts on the table today. Negative cash flow, no current product, paying 10% interest on debt, insiders are selling, not buying, massive ongoing dilution, and a competitor with deeper pockets who is already eating their lunch. It brings us back to Warren Buffett, doesn't it? It always does. Rule number one, don't lose money. Virgin Galactic, as it stands today, violates rule number one. This is speculation, not investment. There is no margin of safety here. You are paying $227 million for a company that might not exist in 18 months. So, the [...] interestingly, they just cut their price target way down to $8. So why hold? Why not sell if the cash situation is so obviously bad? Because they're waiting to see if the Delta class actually exists. There's no revenue to model right now. There's no PE ratio because there's no ENO earnings. They are valuing a story, a narrative, and until that story is proven false or true until that story...
